data.title: Twilio Is Cheap For A Reason - Reversal Remains Prolonged
data.description:
TWLO Is Inherently Undervalued - Offering Opportunistic Investors With The Chance Of A Great Upside Potential

We previously covered Twilio (NYSE:TWLO) in February 2023, discussing its robust prospects as more enterprises sought to integrate ChatGPT-like capability into their tech offerings across SMS, online messaging, voice calls, emails, and contact centers, amongst others.

Combined with the projected near-term profitability, the potential boost in AI SaaS demand, and the ongoing restructuring, we had believed that the stock's investment thesis remained compelling upon a moderate retracement to our recommended entry range at between $55s and $60s.

TWLO YTD Stock Price

Since then, TWLO has had a volatile performance as it bounces off the Q4'22 and H1'23 bottom of $40s, before trading sideways for most of H2'24 and H1'24.

Much of the pessimism embedded in its stock prices is attributed to the decelerating growth after the hyper-pandemic periods, further restructuring by December 2023, and the sudden management change by January 2024, worsened by the volatilities attributed to activist investors and the system breach in June 2024.

Combined with the slower generative AI roll-out and uncertain restructuring/ monetization, we can understand why the market has been pessimistic about TWLO's prospects, as observed in the stock's mixed YTD performance compared to its communication SaaS peers.

On the other hand, we believe that the sell-off has been overly done indeed, since International Data Corporation [IDC] continues to report TWLO's undisputed position as the clear global Communications Platform as a Service [CPaaS] leader with a 24.1% market share as of September 2023.

With the CPaaS market expected to grow at an accelerated CAGR of +15.8% from $14.3B in 2022 to $29.7B in 2026, we believe that the company remains well positioned to re-accelerate growth once its newer AI offerings take off.

If anything, TWLO has already launched numerous new products, including Voice Intelligence, Traffic Optimization Engine, Branded Calling, SendGrid Engagement Quality, and Fraud Guard, leveraging on its in-house generative AI platform, CustomerAI.

Early product adoption seems to be promising as well, as the "newer higher-margin software products (Verify and Voice Intelligence) ... rapidly gain adoption," as the company also launches the "personalized virtual agent into private beta" in Q2'24 - with these new products likely to drive increased cross-selling.

The same has been observed in its growing multi-year remaining performance obligations of $152.6M (+1.3% QoQ/ +23.6% YoY), implying its ability to increasingly monetize its existing consumer base despite the slowing Active Customer Account growth and flat Net Dollar Retention Rate [NDR].

At the same time, TWLO has already partnered with OpenAI, leveraging on the latter's flagship multilingual and multimodal GPT-4o model for OpenAI Realtime API by early October 2024, allowing the former to further drive growth opportunities from Q4'24 onwards.

With the worst of the growth headwinds likely behind us, we believe that the company's prospects may sequentially improve moving forward, as observed in the stickiness of its offerings and growing usage-based revenues by +4.4% YoY.

The Consensus Forward Estimates

As a result of the management's raised FY2024 guidance, we can understand why the consensus have moderately raised their forward estimates, with TWLO expected to generate an accelerated top/ bottom-line growth at a CAGR of +6.7%/ +20.1% through FY2026.

This is compared to the previous estimates of +8.4%/ +14%, while building upon the historical growth at +44.9%/ +86% between FY2018 and FY2023, respectively.

TWLO Valuations

This is also why we believe that TWLO is attractive at FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 20.91x, compared to its 5Y mean of 828.04x and the sector median of 24.31x.

Given the double-digit adj EPS growth prospects, its FWD PEG non-GAAP ratio of 0.70x appears relatively cheap as well, compared to the 5Y mean of 9.62x and the sector median of 1.89x.

Even when comparing against TWLO's communication/ cloud/ API peers, including Salesforce, Inc. (CRM) at FWD PEG non-GAAP ratio of 1.79x, Snowflake (SNOW) at 8.54x, and Sinch AB (OTCPK:CLCMF) at 10.21x, it goes without saying that the former remains highly compelling at these discounted levels - with it offering interested investors with an improved margin of safety.

So, Is TWLO Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

TWLO 5Y Stock Price

For now, sentiments surrounding TWLO's prospects have lifted tremendously, as the new management talked up the reacceleration of its bottom-line growth during the recent Goldman Sachs Communacopia + Technology Conference:

Like, in terms of financial discipline, we took a company that just a couple of years ago was losing money, consuming cash and is now mid-teens operating margins, non-GAAP, and with a marker out on GAAP profitability for next year, have reduced our stock-based compensation considerably and have a path to continue doing so, generating a lot of cash and we feel really good about that. (Seeking Alpha)

With the management also guiding "material margin expansion in our P&L" over the next few years, we can understand why the market has gotten exuberant surrounding TWLO's prospects, as observed in the stock price rally by +23.2% since the September 2024 bottom.

Based on the LTM adj EPS of $3.11 (+223.9% sequentially) and the FWD P/E valuations of 20.91x, it appears that the stock is trading at a slight premium from our estimated fair value of $65.

Even so, based on the consensus raised FY2026 adj EPS estimates from $3.88 to $4.25, there remains an excellent upside potential of +25.9% to our long-term price target of $88.90 as well.

If anything, TWLO continues to deploy part of its robust Free Cash Flow generation at $826.6M over the LTM (+475.2% sequentially) to shareholder returns, with 7.2% of its float already retired over the LTM and another $800M expected to be completed by the end of the year.

As a result of the robust capital appreciation and shareholder return prospects, we are rating the TWLO stock as a Buy.

Risk Appetite

For now, the stock market appears to be increasingly greedy, with elevated McClellan Volume Summation Index at 1,700.45x compared to neutral levels of 1,000x, and rising CBOE Volatility Index at 18.37x compared to the start of the year at 13.20x.

With the stock market also entering the Q3'24 earning season, readers may want to brace for some near-term volatility indeed, depending on how the overall communication market performs.

With TWLO set to report its FQ3'24 earnings results on October 30, 2024, readers may want to compare its upcoming execution to the management's revenue guidance of $1.08B at the midpoint (in line QoQ/ +4.8% YoY) and adj EPS guidance of $0.835 (-1% QoQ/ +43.9% YoY).

Assuming another beat performance, we may see the management further raise its FY2024 guidance as it has over the past two quarters, with the growing optimism also observed in the consistently raised consensus adj EPS estimates.

Readers will be well advised to monitor TWLO's key performance metrics across the Active Customer Account and NDR from FQ2'24 levels of 316 (+1% QoQ/ +3.9% YoY) and 102% (in line QoQ/ -1 points YoY) as well, respectively, with any acceleration in growth likely to be well received, supporting the recovery in its stock prices.

Otherwise, we are likely to see the stock experience a deep pullback, as it has after the supposed FQ1'25 guidance miss in February 2024.

At the same time, while we are cautiously optimistic about TWLO eventual reversal, investors may want to temper their near-term expectations, especially since they have only begun to launch new AI capabilities over the past few quarters, with monetization likely to be slow in the first year.

As a result, it goes without saying that the stock is only suitable for patient investors with a long-term investing trajectory, since its reversal is likely to be prolonged.

data.title: CAVA: Can Its EPS Grow At A 20%+ CAGR For 20 Years? If Not, Don't Buy
data.description:
CAVA Group (NYSE:CAVA), which operates Cava Grill restaurants, has gained 311% in the past year. I believe the business has lots of potential as it achieves economies of scale. There are several things I like about CAVA, including its relatively high gross profit margin for its size (compared to competitors) and its steady improvements in profitability.

Furthermore, it has recently become profitable on a free-cash-flow basis and is still growing at a rapid pace, with plenty of more room to grow, as it isn't nearly as big as Chipotle Mexican Grill (CMG). Things are looking bright for CAVA.

However, its valuation prices in too much optimism at the moment. I think the company will need to grow its EPS by at least 20.8% annually over the next 20 years to see half-decent (but still not impressive) returns. As a result, I rate CAVA stock as a Hold.

Rising Profitability And An Impressive Gross Profit Margin

CAVA has a TTM gross profit margin of 37.5%. Before you flame me for calling that impressive, allow me to explain. I can think of two main publicly-traded competitors to CAVA that cater to the health-conscious consumer. Those are Chipotle and Sweetgreen (SG).

Sweetgreen's TTM gross profit margin is a relatively low 19.6%. Meanwhile, Chipotle has a higher TTM gross margin of 41% -- a bit higher than CAVA's. However, it's also much larger, as it has generated $10.663 billion in revenue in the past 12 months, while CAVA's TTM revenue is about $845.2 million.

Looking back in the past 10 years, Chipotle had a 37.8% gross profit margin in Fiscal 2014, when its full-year revenue was $4.11 billion.

In other words, CAVA has been able to scale to achieve the same gross profit margin at a much smaller size. Plus, its gross profit margin has only been expanding, with last quarter's margin at 38.7%.

CAVA's EBITDA margin has also grown in the past few years, with it hitting 13.2% in the past quarter and 10.4% in the past 12 months. That's roughly where CMG's EBITDA margin was in the late 2010s.

Room For More Margin Expansion

If we use Chipotle as an example (I know Chipotle's food is not Mediterranean, but there are overlaps in ingredients and the business models are similar), then CAVA has room to grow its margins. For instance, CMG's gross profit margin of 41% and its TTM EBITDA margin of 20.1% suggest that CAVA can possibly reach these levels as well.

Given how quickly CAVA has been able to scale its margins, I wouldn't be surprised if it passes those levels within a few years.

CAVA is clearly focused on efficiency, and the management's comments from previous earnings calls highlight that. In the Q1 earnings call, management mentioned its labor deployment test, which is "net-neutral from a labor dollars and labor hours standpoint," according to the firm's CEO. The focus of the test is to reallocate employee "hours to deliver better food, better hospitality, and more efficient speed of service," ensuring that employees are at the right place at the right time.

When the Q1 results came out, 30 restaurants were part of this pilot program. Then, in the Q2 earnings call, management stated that it expected over 75 restaurants to be part of the program by early fall. There will be a company-wide rollout next year if things go as expected.

Next, there's the Connected Kitchen initiative, which uses AI to help make operations more efficient. Regarding this, here's what the CEO said:

We're currently running a small pilot of AI video technology that monitors how quickly ingredients on the in-restaurant make line are being depleted and alerts the team in real time for prep and cook batch amounts. The system is in the learning phase, and we expect it to go live in pilot restaurants in early fall. We'll update you on the initial results in our next earnings call. While this initiative is still in the very early stages, we believe it can drive quality and consistency, increase order accuracy, boost speed of service and simplify prep and planning.

These efficiency efforts, combined with economies of scale from a growing business (revenue growth is expected at over 20% for both Fiscal 2025 and 2026), should allow CAVA's margins to keep expanding.

Free Cash Flow Is Now Positive

CAVA's cash from operations metric has been positive since the July 2022 quarter, but free cash flow was negative up until the past two quarters. With cash from operations rapidly outpacing capital expenditures, free cash flow can grow notably from here, which is what happened recently. In Q2, FCF was $22.7 million, while in Q1, it was $4.7 million. However, in Q1, interest income was $4.9 million (with $0 in interest expense), which means that FCF would be negative if it wasn't for that. Thus, Q2 was the first quarter of true operating FCF, in my view, and that's great to see.

For 2024, the consensus from two analysts suggests that CAVA will see FCF of $27.655 million, while the 2025 estimate is $51.67 million (per tikr.com). Stock-based compensation is also relatively low, at least for now, at $2.95 million last quarter, so that's not affecting the valuation too much.

The Problem Is The Valuation

I couldn't find any fundamental problems with CAVA, as you probably could tell from my analysis so far. Of course, the issue is the valuation. Below, you can see the forward EPS estimates. I'd take the 2026 estimates with a grain of salt because they come from only two analysts, but for 2024, it has a forward P/E of ~338x, and for 2025, the forward P/E is 272.4x.

Look at it this way. Its TTM EPS is $0.37 (or $0.366 if you want to be more precise). This gives it a TTM P/E ratio near 373x, which is not sustainable. At the same time, CMG's P/E ratio is roughly 57x. That itself is arguably overvalued, but let's use it in this example regardless.

Assume that 20 years from now, when CAVA is hopefully closer to Chipotle's current size, CAVA ends up with a P/E ratio of 57x (although I think this is still incredibly optimistic). In order to achieve a ~10% CAGR in those 20 years, CAVA stock will need to rise to $918.71 from its current price of $136.56. In order for that to happen, with a P/E of 57x, its EPS would have to be ~$16.117.

In other words, EPS would need to grow at a CAGR of 20.83% over the next 20 years, equivalent to a 44-fold increase. That's a lot of growth to factor in. And that's only to get a 10% CAGR using a high P/E ratio of 57x. If the P/E ratio dropped to a more normal level like 30x (a bit higher than the P/E ratios of Domino's (DPZ) and McDonald's (MCD)), then the annual shareholder returns would be just 6.53%.

I'm not necessarily saying that CAVA can't see very high and continuous growth from here, as the food market is huge, but there are many things that have to go right for the company in order for shareholders to see good returns.

The Bottom Line On CAVA Stock

I really like CAVA as a company, as its financials seem to be trending in the right direction. Revenue is expected to keep growing at a strong pace, the company is now free-cash-flow positive, and profit margins are rising. In addition, its gross profit margin is quite strong when compared to CMG and SG, relative to the size of the company.

I believe that its profit margins are set to continue growing due to efficiency efforts and economies of scale. After all, CAVA still has room to catch up to CMG's margins. This should support strong EPS growth over the next few years.

However, at nearly 338x 2024 earnings estimates, I just can't buy the stock. There's a lot of growth priced in, as mentioned in my valuation section, and it almost feels as if the stars have to align in order for CAVA to see 10% returns per year. Thus, I rate it as a Hold.

data.title: 2 New Publicly Traded BDCs With Top-Class Defense
data.description:
Starting from the moment, when the Fed decided to aggressively hike the base rates, many banks have suffered notable capital outflows leading to even several insolvencies such as in the case of Silicon Valley Bank and First Republic Bank. This in combination the challenges at the traditional banking front with increased regulations have created an opportunity for private credit actors to step in and offer solutions.

As a result of the favorable fundamental backdrop and higher base rates, which are inherently positive for private credit players, there has been a sizeable capital allocation shift into the private credit segment. For example, such large institutional giants as KKR & Co. Inc. (KKR), Brookfield Corporation (BN), and Blue Owl Capital (OWL) have all outlined very ambitious plans for growing their private credit portfolios.

Consequently, over the past two to three years, there have been quite many IPOs in the BDC space, where the private asset managers have offloaded parts of their portfolios, nicely profiting from favorable valuations.

In 2024 alone, four new BDCs have been made public, which in the context of the sector's size (i.e., now roughly 45 players) could be deemed as a meaningful addition.

In this article, I will elaborate on two of them, which, in my opinion, carry very solid fundamentals that clearly substantiate a case for being included in prudent BDC dividend investor portfolios.

Pick #1 - Morgan Stanley Direct Lending Fund

Even though Morgan Stanley Direct Lending Fund (MSDL) was sent to the public just in January this year, it already ranks as one of the largest BDCs in the entire sector, carrying over $1.8 billion in NAV.

Currently, MSDL offers a dividend yield of 9.9%, which is backed by truly robust fundamentals.

Before I explain this in more detail, let's take a look at its share price performance so far. As we can see in the chart below, MSDL has underperformed the market due to massive drop in early July.

Here the thing is that the registered decline was purely driven by the end of post-IPO lock-up period for the internal shareholders to sell their stakes in the market. Namely, the share price correction was not driven by weak financials, but rather by an increased supply of shares in the public market.

Going forward, there will be an additional expiry of the lock-up period in January 2025, which theoretically could send the stock price lower again. However, I do not think that this will be the case given that currently (as a result of the notable correction) MSDL no longer trades at a premium over NAV. Instead, based on the current share price, MSDL has a slight discount to NAV, which introduces an inherent protection as to how low the performance-unrelated market cap decline can go.

Having said, what should be more important to long-term BDC dividend investors is the underlying fundamentals of MSDL. Here I would like to emphasize four aspects.

First, MSDL's portfolio is very diversified across different industries and more than 170 borrowers. More than 95% of the sector-level exposure is related to non-cyclical industries. Plus, the lion's share of portfolio's asset base is located in senior secured first lien credit, where in almost all cases there is a presence of PE-sponsored capital. These are the safest exposures in the BDC sector (i.e., it is not possible to find a more defensive investment case than senior secured first lien with a PE-backed capital).

Second, the portfolio companies are healthy with well-capitalized balance sheets. For example, as of Q2, 2024, the weighted average loan-to-value of MSDL's portfolio companies stood at ~ 40% and the weighted average EBITDA generation was at $150 million, which is a clear indicative of high quality and already greatly established businesses.

Third, and this is very important, as of Q2, 2024 MSDL had a debt to equity of 0.86x, which is significantly below the sector average of ~ 1.16x. Having so strongly capitalized balance sheet means that MSDL can do two things: 1) accommodate portfolio growth, and thus boost the earnings generation without impairing the financial profile, and 2) avoid magnified consequences (and be in a better position than most BDCs) in case of further non-accruals.

Fourth, even though MSDL has a relatively overcapitalized balance sheet and a pure play focus on senior secured first lien investments, which per definition produce lower yields than riskier investment alternatives, the base dividend coverage level is still quite solid. Based on the recent financial and dividend, the distribution coverage level stands at 126%, which again is better than the sector average of ~ 115%.

Pick #2 - Kayne Anderson BDC

Kayne Anderson BDC, Inc. (KBDC) is another high quality BDC, which has been made public in May 2024. It offers a similar yield to MSDL (i.e., 9.8%), but has not experienced negative price correction due to the expiries of post-IPO lock-up periods. There will three expiry dates: November 5, 2024, February 3, 2025, and May 9, 2025. Yet, since it trades at P/NAV of 0.97x, I do not see a risk of a major correction in any of these of the IPO-lock updates.

With that being said, I would recommend investors to wait for the release of the first lock-up restriction on November 5, 2024, just to see how the market reacts, and most importantly, to avoid the slightly elevated risk of some correction, which could stems from the first lock-up expiry date as usually the first expiries are the most volatile ones. Plus, the opportunity cost of waiting a couple of days is not that significant, as in MSDL's case.

After that, I would recommend defensive BDC dividend investors to seriously consider opening a slight exposure in KBDC. In my opinion, it is just as strong (if not even stronger) as MSDL. Namely, the chances of experiencing a distribution cut down the road are extremely limited.

Here are also four distinct aspects, which justify such a stance.

First, almost 99% of the total KBDC's exposure lies in the senior secured first-lien loan segment, with almost all the investments backed by PE sponsored equity capital. The diversification is also there, as KBDC has ~ $1.2 billion of NAV, which is meaningfully above the sector median of $840 million.

Second, at the underlying portfolio company level the credit statistics look rather solid, and I would even consider them as arguably one of the best in the sector. For example, the weighted average leverage ratio for the portfolio companies stands at 4.1x, and the weighted average interest coverage is at 3.2x. The typical levels we can find among other relatively large size BDCs are around 5x to 6x for leverage and 1.6x to 2.2x for interest coverage. In other words, there is a notable positive gap in the context of KBDC's positions.

Third, KBDC has the lowest leverage in the entire sector. Currently, the debt to equity level is 0.52x, which is clearly a suboptimal state. We should expect that the Management will gradually assume incremental debt to boost the income generation and achieve a balance between equity and debt on the books. This will, inevitably, lead to earnings growth as KBDC funds new opportunities via cheaper debt.

Fourth, just as in MSDL's situation, the distribution coverage for KBDC is high, especially if we factor in the overcapitalized balance sheet and focus on high-quality assets. Currently, the base dividend coverage is 127%, which leaves plenty of room to fund growth by using not only debt, but also internally retained equity.

The bottom line

As described above, both MSDL and KBDC are interesting BDCs, which should be considered by defensive BDC investors. In my opinion, both of them carry similar financial characteristics (if not even better) than Blackstone Secured Lending Fund (BXSL), which is commonly deemed as one of the safest BDCs out there.

The key risk what we have to take into account is related to the forthcoming IPO lock-up periods, which could trigger downside volatility irrespective of how solid financials these BDC possess or deliver in the upcoming financial reports.

Here, investors have to decide whether is it worth waiting for the IPO lock-ups to fully expire or investing now, thus avoiding the potential opportunity cost of safely collected dividends and / or an upside move. Given that both BDCs trade at a slight discount to NAV, I do not think that there will be a significant correction.

In a nutshell, both MSDL and KBDC are worth at least putting on a radar to open exposure now or potentially later after the end of IPO-lock up periods.

data.title: Amplitude: Expecting Growth To Accelerate, But Valuation Is Still Not Compelling
data.description:
Investment thesis

Amplitude (NASDAQ:AMPL) offers a leading analytics platform that has gained significant traction in the market, as reflected by its impressive customer base. Recently, the company has faced headwinds, with existing clients reducing their spending to adjust for overspending during the COVID-19 pandemic. However, this trend appears to be behind them now, and revenue growth is poised to accelerate in the coming quarters, driven by the launch of new features and a stronger product-led growth strategy. I anticipate FCF margins will rise sharply, from 9% in Q2 to nearly 15% within the next two years. Despite this positive outlook, I find the current valuation of an EV/Revenue multiple of 3 less compelling, particularly due to elevated levels of stock-based compensation ("SBC"). Therefore, I rate AMPL shares as a Hold.

Key insights into the business

Amplitude is a product analytics platform that helps its customers optimize their digital products to better engage with their users. In addition to analytics, it offers customer data, session replays, and the ability to run experiments to refine the user experience. This positions Amplitude as a comprehensive end-to-end solution, as illustrated in the figure above. The company was recently named the only leader by Forrester (FORR) for Feature Management and Experimentation.

In Q2, revenue grew 8% year over year to $73.3 million, with Annual Recurring Revenue also increasing by 8% to reach $290 million. Despite the company demonstrating solid momentum in upselling its new features and adding large customers, it continues to face headwinds from some tech clients grappling with funding challenges, while others are optimizing their spending after overestimating demand coming out of the COVID period in 2021 and 2022. Consequently, Dollar-Based Net Retention Rates (DBNRR), which have historically remained above 110%, have now dropped to 96% by the end of Q2.

The company posted a FCF margin of 9% in Q2, despite still being unprofitable on a non-GAAP basis. With gross margins reaching 75.6%, the business has inherently strong operating leverage, positioning it for substantial profitability improvements once revenue growth accelerates in the coming quarters.

Expectations for Q3 and beyond

As shown above, management's latest guidance points to revenue of $296 million at the midpoint, implying a growth of 7% versus 2023. Deducting the revenue in H1 2024 of $146 million from the full year revenue expectation, leads to $150 million expected for H2 2024. This implies a year-over-year growth rate of 6% versus the year ago period. It’s important to note that the H2 2024 revenue estimates account for approximately $4 million in headwinds due to lost revenue from Russian sanctions. Without this impact, revenue growth would have accelerated to 9%. Additionally, I believe the guidance provided is relatively conservative, establishing a low bar considering the company’s new CFO has just taken on the role.

Looking ahead, I anticipate that organic growth will benefit from an influx of new customers and increased upselling. The recent launches of Amplitude Plus earlier this year and Amplitude Made Easy in September are designed to streamline the onboarding process for smaller customers. On the upselling front, newer features like Session Replay, introduced earlier this year, are expected to drive additional revenue. Upselling additional features to customers while enhancing DBNRR will also make them less likely to leave the platform, thereby reducing churn. Moreover, I expect DBNRR to improve from the 96% reported in Q2, as management grows increasingly confident that many of the challenges related to previous contract downsizing are behind them. This momentum should support revenue growth rates in the low double-digit range, which I believe the business can achieve by the second half of next year.

The acquisition of Command AI completed earlier this month brings in an additional feature to its platform which can be upsold to existing customers. Through the integration of Command AI into its platform, Amplitude can leverage its insights to make them actionable to further assist the user and improve the overall experience. Though the purchase price was not disclosed, it has been reported to be above $45 million, with Command AI's revenue estimated to be approximately $3.3 million this year.

As the company increasingly shifts towards a product-led growth strategy through Amplitude Plus and Amplitude Made Easy, I expect sales and marketing expenses as a percentage of revenue to gradually decline. I therefore see a path for FCF margins to rise to 15% within the next two years, compared to the 9% margin achieved in Q2. The increased FCF generation expected together coupled with the company's strong balance sheet should allow management to shortly announce a share repurchase program, aimed at offsetting the dilutive impact from the elevated levels of SBC.

AMPL stock valuation

At the current share price of $9, the company has a market cap of $1.18 billion, assuming 131 million shares outstanding. Given my expectation for the company to end this year with a net cash position of $300 million, its enterprise value stands at $0.88 billion. Based on management's latest guidance for $296 million in revenue for FY24, shares are valued at an EV/Revenue multiple of almost 3. This valuation is comparable to PROS Holdings (PRO), which is non-GAAP profitable and expected to grow at around 10% annually, trading at an EV/Revenue multiple of 3.1. Another relevant peer Yext (YEXT), which has higher operating margins but slower growth in the mid single-digit range, is trading at an EV/Revenue multiple of approximately 2.

Given that Amplitude is in the early stages of enhancing its profitability, I further analyze the valuation by assuming it achieves 15% FCF margins this year. This would translate to an FCF estimate of $44 million, implying that shares are trading at a Price/FCF multiple of 26.7. While this may seem reasonable considering the company's improved growth outlook, the valuation becomes much less attractive when factoring in SBC. Currently, SBC expenses are very high, running at an annualized rate of approximately $88 million as shown below, which is about 30% of this year's expected revenue. As a result, I do not find the valuation compelling enough at the current price.

Risks to consider

Amplitude faces competition from much larger players, including Google’s (GOOG) Analytics 360, Salesforce’s (CRM) Tableau, and Adobe (ADBE) Analytics, all of which provide effective analytics solutions that can be bundled with their core product offerings. The market also includes smaller, specialized competitors like Pendo. Amplitude distinguishes itself by offering a comprehensive platform with a wide range of features that spans analytics to actionable insights, complemented by essential integrations with data sources such as Snowflake (SNOW). Additionally, its professional services enhance customers' ability to utilize its tools effectively.

While the company is currently leveraging AI to its advantage, investors should be mindful that AI could potentially replace some of Amplitude's services by automating data analysis and delivering intuitive insights, allowing customers to manage their analytics in-house with greater efficiency.

Finally, a weaker macroeconomic environment could lead to lower retention rates among existing customers and longer sales cycles with new clients, which may adversely affect the company’s growth prospects.

Conclusion

I believe the business is well-positioned to demonstrate accelerating revenue growth as we approach 2025. With strong operating leverage, there is ample opportunity for margins to rise significantly over the next two years. However, I do not find the current valuation compelling, particularly due to the elevated level of SBC.

data.title: Ally Financial: I Was Wrong About The NIM Recovery
data.description:
Around 50 days ago, I rated the stock of Ally Financial (NYSE:ALLY) as a "hold." Back then, I saw an optimistic outlook as lowering interest rates might be the catalyst to consolidate a NIM trough and boost credit originations. However, the valuation didn't add up, and I opted to downgrade my previous "buy" rating to a "hold" due to its unjustifiable trading price.

A few days after my rating, the stock completely plummeted, decreasing -17.62% in a single trading day. This was due to an update from the prior guidance, where management went from expecting a 10 bps rise in NIM to actually expecting a decline.

In hindsight, the decline materialized after Q3 earnings as the NIM contracted by a modest five bps, but it was still higher than in Q1 (3.22% vs 3.13%), keeping the NIM trough narrative intact. Furthermore, based on their year-end expectations of NIM, Ally should see a slight gain in Q4, sitting at 3.25% and increasing from a year ago. So far, this guidance looks interesting, but later on, I will mention some of the management comments that completely change the expectations for a NIM recovery.

Another aspect that caused the stock to drop substantially back then was the CFO's comments that Ally's credit challenges had intensified, with auto loan delinquencies and net charge-offs rising above expectations in July and August. Again, in hindsight, this ended up occurring. In Q3, the 30+ day past-due loans as a percentage of total loans amplified 13 bps and net charge-offs by 24 bps.

Q3 24 Q2 24 Q1 24 Q4 23 Q3 23
NIM 3.22% 3.27% 3.13% 3.17% 3.24%
Ending loan balance ($) 137,501 138,783 137,960 139,439 140,260
30+ Accruing DPD % 2.82% 2.69% 2.43% 2.76% 2.47%
Net charge-off rate 1.50% 1.26% 1.55% 1.77% 1.31%

Source: ALLY Q3 Financial Supplemental

Personally, when waiting for the results, I was expecting a more aggressive downward change in NIM and asset quality based on the CFO comments and also on the stock's -17.62% drop reaction. Looking at the numbers, although these aspects deteriorated and NPL is high, they weren't too far away from the previous quarter, as seen in the table above. Nevertheless, management increased NCO guidance from a mid-point of 1.48% to 1.53%.

Ally's Outlook Suggests a Volatile NIM Recovery

In my previous analysis of Ally, I mentioned how Q1 '24 appeared to be the tipping point of the downward trend of the net interest margin. Although this still holds, based on the Q3 '24 figure, management left some guidance that didn't leave me too optimistic about the NIM outlook.

During the Q3 conference call, CEO Michael Rhodes and CFO Russ Hutchinson emphasized that Ally's balance sheet is liability-sensitive in the midterm and that rate cuts would likely positively affect its NIM.

Nonetheless, they mentioned that the journey to return to their NIM normalized target of 4% (currently at 3.22%) would be pretty volatile. This is because they currently have significant receive floating swaps that would lose yield when benchmark interest rates go down in the cycle, affecting the left side of the net interest income formula. Last, the CFO concluded that Ally has an asset-sensitive balance sheet in the near term due to the swaps, making them vulnerable to interest rate cuts.

"The decline in benchmark rates as the Fed continues to move rates lower will be a tailwind over the medium term given the liability-sensitive nature of our balance sheet. But as we've covered previously, we are modestly asset-sensitive in the near term as floating rate assets and hedges will contractually reprice faster than deposits. We expect to achieve our medium-term NIM target of 4%, but the rapid change in Fed funds implied by the forward curve will create some volatility in the next few quarters if those cuts materialize." Russ Hutchinson

Yet, every cloud has a silver lining; in this case, it is Ally's asset quality. 2022 and 2023 cohorts of auto loans have been challenging, bringing high delinquencies, partly due to elevated interest rates and low levels of personal savings. Nonetheless, over time, Ally has strengthened its credit requirements to the point that first, loan originations declined, and second, the S-tier quality of its clients has considerably weighed more on total loans. With this, the company believes charge-offs and delinquencies would normalize going forward.

"Our move up in credit was most pronounced in 2023 with our S-Tier mix increasing from around 25% in prior years to more than 40%. And within the past year, we've seen an increase in FICO. Also, you can see our PTI took a step down from 2022 to 2023 and again over the last year. We continue to be more selective in what we're putting on the balance sheet. The continued tightening gives us confidence our loss rate will decline over time.

The very early signs on the 2024 vintage are encouraging. As we move past peak losses on the 2022 vintage, we expect the rate of change in delinquency and charge-offs to continue to move lower and ultimately decline on a year-over-year basis." Russ Hutchinson

Ally: From Overvalued to Fairly Valued

Of course, the valuation has entirely shifted with the price drop since my previous analysis. In over a month, the price-to-tangible book value has moved from an overvalued 1.12x to a fair valuation of 0.85x. Over the past five years, the average P/TBV multiple of Ally has been 0.95x, making the valuation appealing and full of opportunity as it is below its average.

Finally, based on CAPM, Ally's cost of equity is 9.9%. When comparing this to the most recent return on core tangible common equity of 13.1%, the stock might be justified in trading above book value. In this case, Ally is trading slightly lower, making a positive aspect that implies an overall fair valuation based on the metrics illustrated.

Conclusion and Stock Rating

After reading the earnings call transcript, my belief in the NIM expansion trajectory changed. I acknowledge that I was wrong since I thought the recovery would be consistent with the monetary policy trajectory, but apparently not in the near term. Unfortunately, slide 8 of the Q3 presentation wasn't displayed in the Q2 presentation, and therefore, I wasn't aware that the swaps were more material than the deposit allocation shift in the near term.

In summary, considering the swaps headwind, a positive outlook for asset quality, and an interesting valuation, I reiterate my "hold" rating on Ally's stock. The NIM outlook was solid in my previous analysis, but the valuation was too expensive. Now, it's the opposite, the valuation seems right, but the NIM recovery would be volatile.

data.title: Dividend Harvesting Portfolio Week 190: $19,000 Allocated, $1,792.47 In Projected Dividends
data.description:
I would have thought the market would have been more volatile heading into the election. Instead, the S&P 500 increased by 0.45% and is hovering around its all-time highs. The Nasdaq was relatively flat as it climbed 0.12%. The election isn't the only major event in the next several weeks, as the FOMC meeting is slated for November 7th. It looks like CME Group is projecting that the Fed will cut by another 25 bps in November and finish the year with another 25 bps reduction in December. The combination of reduced rates and inflation continuing to slow is a great combination for the business landscape in 2025. As the cost of capital and input costs decline, businesses should be able to expand their margins and increase profitability. I believe this will drive higher amounts of EPS and take the market higher, especially if we gain efficiency from AI and robotics. If we end up getting gridlock in Washington, I think there is a good chance that the S&P 500 can close the year above 6,000, which seemed like an impossibility at the beginning of the year. No matter what happens, I will continue to allocate capital and reinvest every dividend that flows into the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio.

I would like to start this paragraph by saying that I do not expect the returns I have experienced over the past 3 months to continue at this rate. I really don't want anyone to think that this will continue and at some point, a retracement will happen. Until then, the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio may continue making all-time highs. After allocating $19,000 to the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio, the account balance finished week 190 at $24,091.21. The account gained $440.21 in profitability and is now up $5,091.21 on invested capital or 26.8%. This week, I added shares of NextEra Energy Partners (NEP) from the list of reader suggestions I have accumulated. The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio generated $27.06 in dividend income this week. The combination of reinvesting the generated income and making these investments increased my forward projected annualized dividend income by $16.68 (0.94%) to $1,792.47. October is turning out to be a solid month for dividend income generation, and I am on the verge of surpassing $1,800 in annual forward dividend income in this account. Things are coming together quite well, and I am excited to see how the remainder of the year plays out. Regardless of whether the markets appreciate or retrace heading into 2025, I believe that the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio will continue to deliver on its investment objectives.

The Overall Performance of the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio

For the first 80 weeks, there wasn't much fluctuation between invested capital and the account value. Then, the Ukraine war and global instability occurred, inflation increased, and the Fed took rates higher at a pace that hadn't been seen since the early 1980s. The portfolio experienced volatility for several weeks, rebounded, and went in and out of profitability for a while. Since week 142, the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio has been in the black, and it feels like every other week, I am seeing new highs in profitability. Over the past 10 weeks, the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio has been in a strong uptrend as it gained 9.02% in profitability and added $1,891.74 on invested capital. I believe that my investment methodology will continue to do well as I am focusing on income-producing investments and reinvesting all the income generated. I am trying to stay below a 5% portfolio weight for individual positions and 20% per sector. My investment goals are to create a portfolio that generates recurring income while mitigating downside risk. Capital appreciation is a secondary goal, as I am not focused on growth at all costs. I would say I am succeeding on my investment goals but can always do better. There have been some bumps along the way, I am in the red on some positions and have exited some investments. I can't get everything correct, but through a broad level of diversification, I believe I can spread my risk across many positions and take advantage of sectors rallying as it occurs.

The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio Dividend Section

Here's how much dividend income is generated per investment basket:

  • Equities $501.05 (27.95%)
  • ETFs $469.29 (26.18%)
  • CEFs $306.09 (17.08%)
  • REITs $305.36 (17.04%)
  • BDCs $210.68 (11.75%)

Collecting dividends can serve many functions in a portfolio. Some investors utilize dividends to supplement their income and live off of them. I'm building a dividend portfolio for myself 30 years into the future. In 2022, I collected $507.80 in dividend income from 533 dividends. In 2023, I collected $978.11 in dividend income from 660 dividends. After 42 weeks in 2024, I have collected $1,231.70 from 567 dividends. This is 126.18% of the total dividend income generated in 2023 from 85.91% of the dividends produced.

There are 10 weeks to go in 2024, and a lot of income has already been generated. After 42 weeks, my weekly average dividend income is $29.33. This is an increase of $12.41 (73.37%) compared to 2023, when I averaged $16.92 per week. I think that I can generate enough income throughout the remainder of 2024 to get my weekly average income in the low $30s. In week 40, the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio produced $89.13 in weekly income, and I think there is a chance that $100 can be generated before the year is over. These stats are going to get more interesting in December, and I look forward to seeing them grow as I continue to add capital during 2025.

October is turning out to be a big month for income, as $104.86 has already been generated. Based on the trending stats, the first month of each quarter has been the largest income producer. This is probably because positions such as Altria Group (MO) and Starwood Property Trust (STWD) pay their dividends during the first month of each quarter. In July, the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio generated $158.06 in dividend income, which was a 10.95% increase from the $142.46 generated in April. If the same trend occurs, then we could see that the dividend income in October will exceed $170. This would set the stage for months that exceed $200 in 2025 and weeks that exceed $100. I am quite pleased with the amount of dividend growth I am experiencing from investing $100 per week and reinvesting all the dividends.

There are now 33 positions that are generating 100% of their share price through their dividends now that Omega Healthcare Investors (OHI) has continued to climb higher. I ended up buying enough NEP this week that it's generating more than 50% of its share price through its dividends. As I look at the table below, I am thinking about which positions I want to add to over the next several weeks. I really want to get at least 5 more positions generating at least 1 share per year from their dividends before the year is over. Eventually, I will get to 40 positions, and then I will work on having 50 positions, all adding shares from the income they generate each year. This will help intensify the powers of compounding as the years progress, and I am excited to see what the future holds.

The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio Composition

I haven't added to REITs in a few weeks, and this week SL Green Realty (SLG) took the sector higher as it shot up by 16.36%. REITs now represent just over 20% of the portfolio, which I had a feeling would occur due to the Fed cutting rates. SLG posted a strong quarter, and shares continue to run. I am trying to even out these sectors, but with some ETFs on the verge of generating at least 1 share per year from their dividends, I may have to direct capital there before I can cool the jets on these investments. One of my goals for 2025 will be to even out how much these sectors represent in the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio.

Individual equities now represent 38.64% of the Dividend Harvesting portfolio while generating 27.95% of the dividend income. REITs, ETFs, CEFs, and BDCs make up 61.36% of the portfolio and generate 72.05% of the forward income. I am working on getting individual equities to represent more of the portfolio. The long-term goal is to have the sectors be close to equal weight, but that may not happen for 5-10 years at this rate, if ever.

The top-10 positions changed a bit as SLG overtook Verizon (VZ) and Enbridge (ENB) for the number 2 spot. STWD and OHI also found their way back into the top-10 holdings as they replaced Ares Capital (ARCC) and JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPQ). MO is down to 4.33%, which is exciting to me, considering that it represented almost 5% of the portfolio about a month ago. I am pleased that the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio is becoming less top-heavy, and even the 20th holding represents 1.82% of the portfolio.

The statistics in the table below have drastically changed since last week. SLG, Realty Income (O), and STWD paid dividends this week, and the top 10 positions changed as ARCC and JEPQ are no longer part of the group. My allocation to the top 10 positions is $5,206.16, and they finished the week with a value of $7,288.54. This is an ROI of $2,082.38 (40%). There has been $752.44 of dividends generated and reinvested, which is 14.45% of the initial investment into these positions. I am now projecting that $510.83 of dividend income will be generated from the top 10 holdings, which have a forward yield of 9.81%. These positions represent 30.25% of the portfolio, while their forward dividend income is projected to generate 28.50% of the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio's annualized income.

Week 190 Addition

In week 190, I took a position that had been suggested multiple times over the past year and finally pulled the trigger as I added NextEra Energy Partners to the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio.

NextEra Energy Partners

  • I recently wrote a dedicated article about NEP which outlined my investment thesis (can be read here)
  • After doing a lot of research on NEP, I think that shares have bottomed, and I am happy to make an investment as shares are trading at a discount to the book value of $37.50
  • Despite cutting its growth forecasts, NEP has been able to continue increasing the dividend each quarter and yields 13.82%
  • According to the EIA's last report, the amount of solar energy that will be produced through 2050 will quadruple as wind energy will more than double
  • I think that renewable stocks will do well, and that NEP is trading at a valuation that makes the risk reward very enticing
  • I plan on adding more to the position in the coming weeks as I think it can rebound as big tech announces earnings
  • If big tech continues to allocate capital at the trajectory they have been doing, I think the utility sector will continue higher and NEP will get closer to trading near its book value

Week 191 Game Plan

In week 191 I am considering adding to my positions in Cisco Systems (CSCO), NEP, and Ford (F).

Conclusion

The suggestions that the readers of this series leave in the comment section are incredible. I follow a lot of companies, and I am continuing to learn about new income investments I didn't know were out there. NEP was one that I had been waiting to execute, and the time finally occurred. The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio is doing well as it made another all-time high and is now producing $1,792.47 in forward dividend income. The chart below indicates what the forward dividend income at the end of each week since I started this portfolio. I become more and more impressed every time I look at this chart to see how much the portfolio has grown by investing $100 per week. Eventually, I will generate enough income to match my allocated capital, and the growth rate will substantially increase. Please leave all your comments below, as I try to interact with everyone in the comment section.

data.title: MAXJ: Good Concept But Buy The September Series
data.description:
Last year, I began covering the Innovator Nasdaq-100 Power Buffer - July (NJUL) and its sibling funds. The Innovator Buffer ETFs are innovative ETFs that promise investors various levels of downside protection over the life of their investment holding periods.

For example, the NJUL ETF uses a protective put-spread collar to protect investors from up to 15% downside for a year from the fund's reset date (Figure 1).

It appears the rapid accumulation of assets by Innovator ETFs (~$20 billion in firm AUM) has caught the attention of the industry behemoth, iShares, as iShares have recently launched their own version of buffer ETFs like the iShares Large Cap Max Buffer Jun ETF (BATS:MAXJ).

With equity market valuations near all-time highs, investors should consider ways to protect their downside, in case markets suffer a large correction in the coming quarters. However, instead of the MAXJ ETF, I recommend investors consider the iShares Large Cap Max Buffer Sep ETF (SMAX), which has a September reset and is trading near the protection level.

Fund Overview

The iShares Large Cap Max Buffer Jun ETF is designed to offer upside participation in the return of the S&P 500 Index while maximizing downside protection against price declines over a 1-year period, reset at the end of each June.

To achieve its mandate, the MAXJ ETF holds the iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV) and a put option with a July 2025 expiry and strike price of $547.23 on the IVV (Figure 2).

The strike price of MAXJ's put option is the closing price of the IVV on June 28th, day 0 in its 'outcome period'. The purchase of MAXJ's protective put option is financed by the ETF selling call options on the IVV ETF with a July 1, 2025 expiry date and strike price of $608.19, roughly 11.1% out-of-the-money ("OTM").

Therefore, taking into consideration the fund's 0.50% management fee, the maximum loss investors will face over the coming year, measured from June 28, 2024, is 0.5%, while the maximum gain is 10.6% (Figure 3).

All Upside, No Downside?

One thing to keep in mind for investors considering the iShares buffer ETFs is that in order to enjoy the full benefit of the fund's protective options, the MAXJ ETF must be held for the entire 'outcome period'. In other words, the MAXJ ETF will only provide full downside protection against drawdowns if investors hold the MAXJ ETF from July 1st, 2024 to June 30th, 2025 of the following year.

If investors buy the MAXJ ETF at other times on the secondary markets, they may experience investment performance that is materially different from the modeled behavior. For example, if investors buy the ETF after a 5% drawdown within the 'outcome period', the MAXJ ETF may actually provide more 'upside' than the 10.6% 'cap'. Similarly, after a significant rally, the MAXJ ETF may provide less than 100% downside protection.

Performing As Expected

So far, the MAXJ ETF has performed as expected, returning 4.3% compared to 7.6% for the IVV (Figure 4).

Importantly, during the early-August market scare when the S&P 500 Index plunged by nearly 10%, the MAXJ ETF protected investors from suffering any unrealized drawdowns.

However, with the strong rally in August and September, the IVV is currently trading at ~$580 / share, which means further upside in the remaining 251-days of MAXJ's holding period may be limited.

Consider SMAX Instead Of MAXJ

For investors worried about elevated market valuations and considering the MAXJ ETF, I would caution them against purchasing the ETF here. As mentioned above, the IVV ETF is currently trading at ~$580 / share, or about halfway between its protective put strike of $547.23 and its returns cap at $608.19. This means investors buying the MAXJ ETF on the secondary markets today will not be protected on the downside from ~$580 to $547.23, should equity markets pullback for an extended period of time until June 30, 2025.

Instead of the MAXJ ETF, I recommend investors consider the iShares Large Cap Max Buffer Sep ETF (SMAX), with the same fund mechanics but resetting in September.

The SMAX ETF's protective puts are struck at $576.82 and its upside cap is at $622.45 (Figure 5).

This means the SMAX ETF is more or less fully protected on the downside until August 31, 2025, while on the upside, there is still ~7% of room for appreciation (Figure 6).

Investors should note that SMAX's total returns buffer is smaller than MAXJ's because at the time of SMAX's inception/reset, implied volatility was lower and thus the SMAX ETF had to sell call options less out-of-the-money in order to fund its protective put.

Current MAXJ investors can even consider selling their MAXJ holdings and buying the SMAX ETF, so they can 'reset' their put protection to $576.82. Of course, if investors sell their MAXJ holdings, they may be liable for capital gains taxes, as the MAXJ ETF has appreciated since June. Investors should consult a tax professional to see whether it makes sense for them to consider switching.

Conclusion

With the S&P 500 Index trading at an elevated 21x Fwd P/E, the highest valuation ratio with the exception of the dot-com bubble and 2021, I believe conservative investors should look for ways to protect their downside exposure, in case markets turn south over the coming quarters (Figure 7).

The buffer ETFs offered by iShares could be an interesting way for investors to fully hedge their downside risks while maintaining modest upside exposure over the coming year.

However, I would not recommend investors buy the MAXJ ETF at this time, as the fund has appreciated ~5% against its June 28 reference price, so buying MAXJ ETF at current levels will not fully protect investors against downside.

Instead, I recommend investors consider the more recent SMAX ETF, which uses a September reset level of $576.82 on the IVV ETF. With the IVV currently trading at around $580 / share, investors buying the SMAX ETF will be almost fully protected from drawdowns until August 31, 2025 while retaining exposure to ~7% of upside returns.

I rate MAXJ a hold and SMAX a buy.

data.title: Quest Diagnostics: Organic Growth Leads To Q3 Earnings Beat
data.description:
In my previous Quest Diagnostics (NYSE:DGX) article, I analyzed the company’s Q2 2024 earnings report, which produced a beat on EPS and revenue. The company did report that their base business revenue grew almost 4%, and their core business volume was up around 3.2%. However, the market reacted negatively to the company’s earnings, and analysts announced downward revisions possibly over market conditions, competition from Labcorp (LH), or challenges from recent acquisitions. Despite the Street’s apprehensions, I was still bullish on DGX, and that was amplified by Quest’s healthy dividend. It has only been about 2 months since that article was posted, and DGX is up roughly 9% and looks to be ready to test the all-time high following strong Q3 earnings. Once again, Quest was able to beat the Street’s expectations for both EPS and revenue… but the market supported DGX, moving the ticker closer to its 52-week high. Now, I am debating adjusting my DGX Buy Targets for the company’s expected growth and the ticker’s new technical state.

I intend to recap Quest’s Q3 earnings and will point out some of the primary highlights for investors. In addition, I will go over the company’s growth outlook and what the Street expects from Quest as we move into 2025. Then, I will present a few risks that investors should consider when managing their position. Finally, I will update my strategy for DGX.

Q3 Review

Quest Diagnostics reported a strong Q3, with revenues increasing by 8.5% to $2.49B, with diluted EPS up 3.6% at $2.30. This beats the Street’s expectations by $0.04 for the EPS and by $70M for the revenue. Quest saw organic growth of 4.2% thanks to a bump in organic requisition volume, leading to an increase in revenue per requisition. In addition, cash flow provided by operations surged 72.5% to $356M, which will help boost Quest's liquidity position. The company credited their performance to their diagnostic segment, which saw its revenue rise 9%. Into the bargain, the company reported successful integration of recent acquisitions, including LifeLabs and Allina Health. In fact, LifeLabs has already contributed approximately $70M in revenue. Additionally, Quest reported growth from their consumer testing, particularly through QuestHealth.com, and partnerships with health plans and hospitals.

The company reported a 3.3% decline in operating income, which they attributed to higher costs and integration expenses. On the other hand, Quest’s adjusted operating income increased by 1.3% to $385M. The company also reported a drop in their operating margin to 13.3%, which is down from 14.9% last year. Quest also revealed that Hurricane Milton is expected to negatively impact Q4 revenues by $15M and EPS by $0.08.

Still, Quest’s Q3 performance encouraged them to raise their 2024 revenue guidance to fall between $9.8B and $9.85B, suggesting a 5.9%-6.5% increase in net revenues. Looking at the EPS, Quest expects their diluted EPS between $7.60 and $7.70 and adjusted diluted EPS in the range of $8.85-$8.95.

Meanwhile, cash provided by operations and cash expenditures are expected to come in line with their prior guidance.

More Growth On The Horizon

The company’s Q3 earnings were boosted by some of their recent acquisitions, which have fueled top-line growth, and these are expected to accelerate growth into 2025. What is more, Quest revealed that they plan to complete eight more acquisitions by year-end, thus, adding more logs to fire and expanding their reach in the healthcare diagnostics industry. Notably, the company expects to launch the Haystack assay from their Haystack Oncology acquisition within the next few months to add another growth driver. 2025 may be a breakout for Quest, as long as they continue to capitalize on their scale, strengthen their diagnostic portfolio, and improve operational efficiencies.

The Street expects Quest to report double-digit revenue growth for Q4 of this year, and will report single-digit growth for the entirety of 2025.

For EPS, the Street is projecting single-digit growth for the next few quarters that will ramp up to double-digit growth starting in Q4 of 2025 which will last until Q3 of 2026.

Zooming out, we can see that Street expects the company to report growth for both revenue and EPS for the next few years.

Indeed, these are just estimates, but they do illustrate how analysts are expecting Quest to recover from the “COVID hangover” and start reporting growth that is fueled by their base business. In fact, Baird recently upgraded DGX to outperform, citing sector trends, core volume growth, and the possibility the company closes more deals. As a result, the firm raised their price target from $157 to $182.

So, I think it is reasonable for investors to have an optimistic outlook on Quest’s growth prospects moving into 2025 with some earnings momentum that could translate to the share price.

Lingering Risks

Even though Quest posted strong earnings with encouraging growth prospects, I must point out a few risks that continue to linger and could influence the company’s performance. First, the global economic environment is not firm with the threat of an economic downturn on the horizon, which might dull the demand for elective procedures and diagnostic testing. Another persistent concern is inflation pressures that could impact wages and the workforce. Furthermore, Quest supply chain disruptions that could end up delaying customer deliveries.

Despite these risks, I will keep my DGX conviction level at 5 out of 5, and the ticker will remain in my “Healthy Dividend” portfolio.

My Plan

In my previous Quest Diagnostics article, I was confident that the company was going to be able to handle their strategic acquisitions, enhance efficiencies, and expand their reach. Although the company still has to deal with some of the same lingering risks, Quest’s Q3 performance and upgraded guidance has solidified both my near-term and long-term bullish conviction in DGX.

At the moment, DGX is trading around $156 per share, which is above my Buy Threshold of $129 per share, but is still below my Sell Target 1 of $205 per share. So, I am still holding off on making any transactions at the moment. However, I am adjusting my targets considering the ticker’s new technical status, and the predicted earnings growth.

DGX’s Daily Chart is still showing a bullish trend, with the share price try to hold above the previous 52-week high. The recent move following the earnings report was supported by elevated RVol and has changed the Go-No-Go indicator from very bearish to neutral. In addition, the share price has climbed above the anchored-VWAP from the previous high, suggesting a change in momentum. This is also supported by a strong change in the RSI. In addition, DGX’s seasonality trends suggest that the last quarter of the year is a bullish period for the year.

As a result, I am updating my Buy Threshold from $129 per share to $150 per share. In addition, I am moving my Sell Target 1 up to $227 per share.

$150 is not far off from the ticker’s current position, so I will have to remain vigilant on DGX’s trading behavior over the rest of 2024 to avoid missing another opportunity to add to my position.

data.title: S&P Equal Weight Vs. Cap Weight
data.description:
The rise of the "mega-caps" in recent years has caused a significant divergence in the performance of the S&P 500 -- which is market cap weighted -- and the S&P 500 Equal Weight index. As the mega-caps like Alphabet (GOOGL), Amazon (AMZN), Apple (AAPL), Meta (META), Microsoft (MSFT), and Nvidia (NVDA) have pushed ever higher into multi-trillion dollar companies, their weight in the S&P 500 has ballooned to more than 25% of the index. In the S&P 500 Equal Weight index, however, where each of the 500 stocks has a 0.2% weighting after quarterly re-balancing, the six mega-caps make up just 1.2% of the index. Given these differences, it's easy to see how the performance between a cap-weighted and equal-weighted index can diverge.

As shown below, the cap-weighted S&P 500 has posted a total return of just over 110% over the last five years, compared to a gain of roughly 84% for the S&P 500 Equal Weight index. As you can see in the chart, the gap between the two didn't really start widening until early on during this bull market in early 2023.

Anecdotally, the cap-weighted version of the S&P 500 seems to be getting all the love these days, with some calling it the perfect momentum index. Combining that recent sentiment with the very clear differences between the two indices, readers may be surprised that the longer-term performance between the two has been similar and even tilts towards the equal-weight version.As shown below, over the last 20 years, the cap-weighted index has just recently overtaken the equal-weighted index on a total return basis, but outperformance has gone back and forth many times over this time frame.

And since 1990, the equal-weighted version of the S&P has actually been the clear winner over the cap-weighted version.

As you can see in the chart below, the Dot Com Bubble of the late 1990s pushed the cap-weighted S&P solidly above the equal-weighted version in the final years of that bubble, but the bursting of the bubble and the 2003-2007 bull market resulted in a performance shift that allowed the equal-weight index to pull ahead. While the mega-caps of today have had a leg up on the rest of the market for the past few years, an extended period of underperformance from them would allow the equal-weighted version to become en-vogue again.

Below is a look at the differences in sector weightings for the S&P 500 (cap-weighted) and S&P 500 Equal Weight indices. By default, the sectors with the largest number of stocks in the index will have the highest weightings in the equal-weight index.

While the Tech sector makes up nearly a third of the cap-weighted index, it's only 13.7% of the equal-weight index. Industrials and Financials each have a larger weight than Tech in the equal-weight index, while they combine for a weighting that's just 2/3 of Tech's weighting in the cap-weighted index.

When looking at the pie charts below, it's the equal-weight version that appears more balanced and diversified at the moment. No sector has a weighting below 4.4% in the equal-weight index, while the cap-weighted index has four sectors with weightings below 3.3%. Movements in Materials, Real Estate, Utilities, and Energy have virtually no impact on the cap-weighted index as a whole these days.
